android_about: App allows internal users to submit forms, receive local and Acoustic push notifications about their submissions, and use the native device camera to scan VINs and take photos of vehicles.
android_about_long: The app is used by dealers and other internal users to submit various forms to the business and receive push notifications when their forms are processed. In addition to entering text, users can use their device camera to scan QR and barcodes of vehicle VINs. They can also take pictures and videos of vehicles and parts.
android_analytics: Implemented Adobe Analytics reporting suite and sent out monthly analytics reports to leadership
android_google_play: Updated Gradle libraries and app functionality to meet Google Play compliance requirements
android_incidents: Managed and troubleshot ServiceNow production incidents opened by end users
android_msauth: Implemented Microsoft Authentication to comply with new security requirements
android_role: Dev lead for Jetpack Compose Kotlin and Java app with 17,000 monthly users
android_role_long: Dev lead for Jetpack Compose Kotlin and Java app with 17,000 monthly users deployed in the United States, Canada, Mexico, Australia, New Zealand, and Egypt.
android_work_architecture: Implemented plugins using MVI architecture with Hilt dependency injection
android_work_build: Fixed issues with CI / CD pipelines run through GitHub Actions
android_work_collab: Collaborated with a team of developers, QA and UAT testers, product owners, and UI/UX to design, size, and develop new features
android_work_deployments: Managed and deployed test builds to Firebase and production builds to Google Play
android_work_responsive: Implemented responsive layouts that met design guidelines for both phones and tablets
android_work_submit: Users are able to submit using email and an asynchronous queue implemented with kotlinx coroutines and jobs. On average, 15,000 - 20,000 submissions are processed per month.
android_work_unitTests: Implemented unit test suite with kotlinx coroutines, JUnit, and MockK

react_about: Application allows internal users to create and modify projects for returning parts and to view tabulated summaries of existing projects
react_role: Typescript developer for Nextjs microfrontend rewrite of legacy application
react_work_api: Implemented Redux RTK Query API for future integration with the server
react_work_forms: Created scalable and robust forms with Formik, validated by Yup
react_work_screens: Created reusable React components and screens based on Figma mockups
react_work_unit_tests: Implemented unit tests using Jest and React Testing Library
statement: Hello! I'm a software developer with {numYears} years experience developing Android and React applications. I enjoy the process of iterating on projects and watching them grow before my eyes. In my spare time, I like to play video games, play and watch soccer, and watch TV. Feel free to reach out if you'd like to get in touch!
arch_struct_explanation: My codebase needs to be intuitive to navigate so I can easily make updates and modifications. It also needs to be scalable, readable, and follow best practices to demonstrate my competency as a developer.
arch_default_description: Click a folder to see its children and a short description. Not every directory or file is represented - that felt excessive!
arch_public_description: The Next.js convention is that anything in the public folder can be referenced anywhere in the codebase starting from the base url /. This is a good place to keep static files.
arch_fonts_description: Here I store all the font files I use in the application as .ttf files. Because they're in 'public', I can access them anywhere in the app.
arch_icons_description: Here I store a sprite file containing SVG icons used by my downloadable resume.
arch_images_description: This folder contains all the images used in my app. This makes it easy for Next.js's Image components to access them - and for me to find them if I need to make an update.
arch_strings_description: This directory stores localization files for each language: en.json, ru.json, etc. If I want to support a new language, I simply add a new json file here.
arch_src_description: Next.js supports the common pattern of placing application code in a 'src' directory to separate it from configuration files (generally in the root directory).
arch_app_description: This is the folder used by the Next.js app router. Every route in the site has to be declared in this directory.
arch_app_architecture_description: This is the folder for the /architecture route - the screen that you're on now! It has its own layout.tsx and page.tsx.
arch_app_disclosures_description: This is the folder for the /disclosures route - which you can view by opening the settings in the top right and clicking on the 'Disclosures' link. It also has its own layout.tsx and page.tsx.
arch_favicon_description: The icon you see on the browser tab. Next.js convention dictates this icon is placed in the app/ directory.
arch_layout_description: Every Next.js route needs a layout.tsx and a page.tsx. Since this is the root layout, it wraps all the other layouts, so in this file I wrap the app content in my theme, localization, and other providers.
arch_page_description: This renders the actual content for the route: in this case, the home route at /. To keep the router lightweight, all I do here is call a react component home.tsx, which is stored in the 'components/home/' directory below.
arch_notfound_description: This is a simple 404 page for any invalid route. Next.js handles this functionality by default, but I created this page so I could have a screen that was consistent with the look and feel of my app.
arch_components_description: This folder contains the React components used to render the various screens of my application. The directory is split into sub-directories by screen and function.
arch_components_architecture_description: For example, this folder contains all the React components specifically for the architecture screen - the screen you're on now.
arch_projectstructure_description: And this file is the specific React Component for the project structure you're viewing now. There are different files for each of the different sections on this screen - given relevant names so it's easy to find and modify them when needed.
arch_constants_description: This folder contains constant variables and lists used throughout the project, split into separate files by functionality. For example, my resume uses resumeConstants.tsx.
arch_constants_arch_description: This file has all the constants used on the screen you're on now. For example, this project structure, an array of MockFile (see the 'types' directory), is defined here. If I wanted to display another folder, I'd add it to the array.
arch_i18n_description: This folder contains a configuration file (request.ts) used to configure my localization library. Both the directory and the file have to have that exact name.
arch_style_description: This folder contains the theming logic for my application. If I need to globally change the look and feel of the site, I will go here.
arch_globals_description: This is a stlying file that needs to be imported in the root directory. Since I'm using MUI and Tailwind for my theming, all I'm doing in this file is importing the tailwind library.
arch_theme_description: In this file, I configure my MUI theme (mainly the light and dark palettes) and I return a ThemeProvider that is wrapped around my application in the root layout.tsx file.
arch_package_description: Various configuration files are placed into the root directory. For example, this file has all the third party npm dependencies that get installed when you run 'npm install'.
arch_types_description: Here I have a types.tsx file where I export all the custom types I use in this project. For example, this simulated project directory is an array of MockFile. A MockFile consists of:
arch_types_desc_title: title: string (The name of the directory or folder. In this case, 'types')
arch_types_desc_ind: indentationLevel: number (Starts at 0, simulates project structure indentation. In this case, 1)
arch_types_desc_desc: description: string (Either the string key directory's description, which fetches the value from the appropriate 'strings' file, or a constant to signify special formatting. In this case, the constant TYPES_DESCRIPTION)
arch_types_desc_isFile: isFile: boolean? (Optional, to show the file icon. Not set for this directory.)
arch_types_desc_path: path: string? (Optional, used to simulate the file explorer behavior by expanding and collapsing appropriate paths)
arch_types_desc_final: Keeping all the types in one directory makes it easy to modify and maintain them. If the project grew large enough, I would split the types file by functionality, similar to how I manage the 'constants' and 'components' directories.
arch_util_description: In this directory, I store utility functions that are used in multiple places in the app. For example, getCookie() and setCookie(), which are used to set the functional MUI and localization cookies.
